message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>COVID-19 pandemic is an extraordinary threat with significant implications in all aspects of human life, therefore, it represents the most immediate challenges for all countries all over the world.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This study is intended to develop a GIS-based analysis model to explore, quantify and model the relationships between COVID-19 morbidity and mortality and their potential predictor variables.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Method</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    For this purpose, a model was developed to estimate COVID-19 incidence and fatality rates in Africa up to 16
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    of August 2020 at the national level. The model involved Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) and Geographically Weighted Regression (GWR) analysis through ArcGIS was applied.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Result</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Spatial Autocorrelation Analysis revealed that there was positive spatial autocorrelation in COVID-19 incidence (Moran index 0.16.
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    value <0.1), and fatality (Moran index 0.0.35,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    value<0.01) rates within different African countries. At continental level, OLS revealed that COVID-19 incidence rate was found to be positively associated with overcrowding, health expenditure, HIV infections and air pollution and negatively associated with BCG vaccine (β=2.97,1.45, 0.01, 3.29, −47.65 respectively,
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    < 0.05) At the same time, COVID-19 fatality was found to be positively related to asthma prevalence and tobacco use. Yet, certain level of inconsistency was noted in the case of COVID-19 fatality, which was negatively related to elder population, poverty, and cardiovascular mortality (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    <0.05). This model showed convenient level of validity in modeling the relationship between COVID-19 incidence as well as fatality and their key predictors using GWR. In this respect, the model explained about 58% and 55% of the variance in COVID-19 incidence and fatality rates, respectively, as a function of considered predictors.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Application of the suggested model can assist in guiding intervention strategies, particularly in case of local and community level whenever the data on COVID-19 cases and predictors variables are available.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: GIS-Based Analysis Framework for identifying COVID-19 Incidence and Fatality Determinants at National Level Case study: Africa

reference.unstructured: ATCTW, Advancing Transfusion and Cellular Therapies Worldwide (2020) AABB’s Coronavirus Resources. AABB. http://www.aabb.org/advocacy/regulatorygovernment/Pages/AABB-Coronavirus-Resources.aspx [Accessed 18 December 2020].

reference.article-title: Geographically Weighted Regression: A Method for Exploring Spatial Nonstationarity

reference.unstructured: Fischer Manfred M. & Arthur Getis (2010). Handbook of Applied Spatial Analysis: Software Tools, Methods and Applications, Springer, Heidelberg.

reference.article-title: Improving death registration and statistics in developing countries: Lessons from sub-Saharan Africa

WHO, The world Health Organization (2020a) The Global Health Observatory.
WHO, World Health Organization (2020b) COVID-19 Weekly Epidemiological Update. WHO, Geneva
